purpose: Tests whether fiscal consolidation attempts can succeed by examining historical base rates and required preconditions (monetary easing, currency depreciation, external demand boom)
components: 0/7 historical base rate for consolidation without at least one offset mechanism
Required offsets: monetary easing >200bp, currency depreciation >10%, external demand boom
Financial repression precondition scorecard (US meets ~1.5 of 5)
0/3 base rate for financial repression with open capital accounts
DOGE realization rate (0.3-0.6x for comparable initiatives)
current_assessment: The US meets none of the three offset conditions: monetary easing is constrained by inflation, currency depreciation is constrained by reserve status, and external demand boom is unavailable in the current global environment. DOGE will underdeliver at 0.3-0.6x. Formal financial repression fails precondition test. The arithmetic implies no viable consolidation path without a significant regime change.
limitations: N=7 is a small sample with heterogeneous country characteristics
The US may be genuinely different from the sample due to reserve currency status
DOGE is sufficiently novel that historical realization rates may not apply
The framework assumes current constraints are binding, but a crisis could change the constraint set
